metadata.title: Why Read-Only Access Still Leaks
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Claude Cowork candidate 09. Source chapter: claude-cowork/chapters/03-connectors-local-files-and-permissions.md. One mechanism: connecting a service usually grants the whole account, not the folder you had in mind — and anything readable can become source material, so the gap between the scope you intended and the scope you granted is exactly the material that shows up where it shouldn't.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = the intended scope (the 3 files); CRIMSON #BF3339 = the granted scope (entire account) and what leaks from it
metadata.note: Exclusions: NO access-ladder seven-row table, NO plugin/MCP scope aside, NO scheduled-task risk, NO computer-use tangent, NO connector-configuration how-to.

beats.narration_text: This is Musinique. Why Read-Only Access Still Leaks.
Three program report PDFs. You connect Google Drive. The summary quotes a draft funder email you never sent.

The tools this video covers were built for enterprises with engineering teams and compliance budgets. The musician in the van at 1 a.m. has access to the exact same machinery, for free, through a browser. The gap between knowing this exists and knowing how to use it is what this channel closes — one working piece of your promotion deck at a time.

artifactLines: The same tools the platforms use — pointed back at you.
Roll your own. Own the deck.

beats.narration_text: Read-only sounds like the safe permission. It means the tool can read — everything in the connected account. The draft email was in the account.
beats.scene_description: a cloud drive interface on a screen — folders visible, many files, professional context
beats.image_prompt: B02, close-up of a computer screen showing a cloud drive folder interface with many files and folders visible, organized but extensive, editorial collage style, desaturated newsprint treatment, warm cream tones, no faces visible

beats.narration_text: You granted read-only access — the most harmless-sounding permission. A never-sent draft email ended up quoted in the report. Why did read-only leak?

document.quote: You granted read-only access. A never-sent draft email ended up quoted in the report. Why did read-only leak?

beats.narration_text: Here is what you intended: three files. Here is what the connector granted: the entire account. The intended scope sits inside the granted scope. The draft email sits in the granted scope.

viz.label: large CRIMSON circle 'entire account'; inside: small TEAL circle '3 files I meant'; outside the TEAL circle but inside CRIMSON: 'draft email' dot in CRIMSON
viz.note: the scope gap — intended vs granted

card.sub: read access = read everything in scope

beats.narration_text: Connecting a service usually grants the whole account, not the folder you had in mind. Anything the connector can read can become source material. The gap between the scope you intended and the scope you granted is exactly the material that shows up where it shouldn't.

viz.label: TEAL 'intended' small circle; CRIMSON 'granted' large circle; an arrow from the gap region (CRIMSON only) → OUTPUT box (CRIMSON); label 'gap → leak'
viz.note: the gap region feeds the leak

beats.narration_text: Read access means read everything in scope. A sensitive document inside the connected account can become source material. Read-only is not safe if the scope is too wide.

document.quote: Read access means read everything in scope. Read-only is not safe if the scope is too wide.

beats.narration_text: Illustrative. Three program report PDFs are the task. Connecting Google Drive exposes four thousand items — grant applications, financial records, a draft funder email. The summary quotes one line from the draft. The user catches it on a final read. Only barely.

viz.label: TEAL: '3 PDFs (task)'; CRIMSON: '~4,000 items in account'; one CRIMSON dot labeled 'draft funder email'; arrow → OUTPUT quotes draft; 'illustrative'
viz.note: THE EXAMPLE — draft email leaks into output

beats.narration_text: The permission was read-only. The scope was the whole account. The leak was the gap between them.

document.quote: The permission was read-only. The scope was the whole account. The leak was the gap between them.

beats.narration_text: Read-only is not a safe permission. It is a safe permission for the right scope.

card.copy:
Read-only is not safe.
It is safe for the right scope.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Claude Cowork series.

beats.name: OutroCTA
beats.narration_text: Like and subscribe for more. From Musinique.
